name: wrangler
context: Install and use the Wrangler CLI — develop, test, deploy, and manage all Cloudflare developer platform services
info: Wrangler is the official Cloudflare CLI for the developer platform. It handles Worker development (init, dev, deploy, tail), KV operations (namespace CRUD, key read/write/list), R2 operations (bucket CRUD, object upload/download), D1 operations (database CRUD, migrations, SQL execution), Pages deployment, secret management, and local development with Miniflare. Wrangler reads configuration from `wrangler.toml` in the project root. Install with `npm install -g wrangler` or as a dev dependency. Authenticate with `wrangler login` (OAuth) or `CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN` environment variable.
body:
# Wrangler CLI

Wrangler is the Cloudflare developer platform CLI. It manages the entire lifecycle: create, develop, test, deploy, and monitor.

## Installation

```bash
# Global install
npm install -g wrangler

# Project dev dependency (recommended)
npm install --save-dev wrangler

# Verify installation
wrangler --version
```

## Authentication

```bash
# Interactive OAuth login (opens browser)
wrangler login

# Verify
wrangler whoami

# Or use API token (CI/CD, agents)
export CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN="your-token"
export CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID="your-account-id"
```

## Core Commands

### Workers

```bash
# Create new Worker project
npm create cloudflare@latest my-worker

# Start local development server
wrangler dev                        # local mode
wrangler dev --remote               # use remote bindings
wrangler dev --port 8080            # custom port

# Deploy to production
wrangler deploy                     # deploy to *.workers.dev
wrangler deploy --env staging       # deploy to named environment

# Stream live logs
wrangler tail my-worker
wrangler tail my-worker --status error
wrangler tail my-worker --format json

# Manage secrets
wrangler secret put API_KEY
wrangler secret list
wrangler secret delete API_KEY

# Rollback
wrangler rollback
```

### KV

```bash
# Namespace management
wrangler kv namespace create MY_KV
wrangler kv namespace create MY_KV --preview
wrangler kv namespace list
wrangler kv namespace delete --namespace-id <id>

# Key operations
wrangler kv key put --namespace-id <id> "key" "value"
wrangler kv key get --namespace-id <id> "key"
wrangler kv key delete --namespace-id <id> "key"
wrangler kv key list --namespace-id <id> --prefix "user:"

# Bulk operations
wrangler kv bulk put --namespace-id <id> data.json
wrangler kv bulk delete --namespace-id <id> keys.json
```

### R2

```bash
# Bucket management
wrangler r2 bucket create my-bucket
wrangler r2 bucket list
wrangler r2 bucket delete my-bucket

# Object operations
wrangler r2 object put my-bucket/path/file --file ./local.txt
wrangler r2 object get my-bucket/path/file --file ./download.txt
wrangler r2 object delete my-bucket/path/file
wrangler r2 object list my-bucket --prefix images/
```

### D1

```bash
# Database management
wrangler d1 create my-db
wrangler d1 list
wrangler d1 delete my-db

# Migrations
wrangler d1 migrations create my-db create_users
wrangler d1 migrations apply my-db --local
wrangler d1 migrations apply my-db --remote
wrangler d1 migrations list my-db --remote

# Execute SQL
wrangler d1 execute my-db --command "SELECT * FROM users" --remote
wrangler d1 execute my-db --file ./seed.sql --remote
```

### Pages

```bash
# Deploy
wrangler pages deploy ./dist --project-name my-site

# Project management
wrangler pages project create my-site --production-branch main
wrangler pages project list

# Secrets
wrangler pages secret put DB_URL --project-name my-site
```

## wrangler.toml Reference

```toml
# Required
name = "my-worker"
main = "src/index.ts"
compatibility_date = "2024-09-23"

# Optional
account_id = "your-account-id"
compatibility_flags = ["nodejs_compat"]
workers_dev = true  # enable *.workers.dev subdomain

# Routes
routes = [
  { pattern = "api.example.com/*", zone_name = "example.com" },
]

# Or custom domain
# routes = [{ pattern = "api.example.com", custom_domain = true }]

# Environment variables
[vars]
ENVIRONMENT = "production"
API_URL = "https://api.example.com"

# KV bindings
[[kv_namespaces]]
binding = "MY_KV"
id = "namespace-id"
preview_id = "preview-namespace-id"

# R2 bindings
[[r2_buckets]]
binding = "MY_BUCKET"
bucket_name = "my-bucket"

# D1 bindings
[[d1_databases]]
binding = "DB"
database_name = "my-database"
database_id = "database-uuid"

# Cron triggers
[triggers]
crons = ["0 * * * *", "*/15 * * * *"]

# Named environments
[env.staging]
name = "my-worker-staging"
vars = { ENVIRONMENT = "staging" }
routes = [{ pattern = "staging-api.example.com/*", zone_name = "example.com" }]
```

## Local Development (Miniflare)

`wrangler dev` uses Miniflare, a local Workers simulator that supports:
- KV, R2, D1 (local persistence in `.wrangler/state`)
- Cron trigger testing via HTTP: `curl http://localhost:8787/__scheduled`
- Hot reloading on file changes
- Breakpoint debugging via `--inspect`

```bash
# Dev with inspector for Chrome DevTools debugging
wrangler dev --inspect

# Clear local state
rm -rf .wrangler/state
```

sections.title: Installation and authentication
sections.body: Install with `npm install -g wrangler` or as a dev dependency. Authenticate with `wrangler login` for OAuth or set `CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN` and `CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID` environment variables. Verify with `wrangler whoami`. For CI/CD, always use environment variables. See [[cloudflare/auth]] for token creation.

sections.title: Local development
sections.body: `wrangler dev` starts a local development server with hot reloading. Uses Miniflare to simulate Workers runtime with local KV, R2, and D1. Add `--remote` to use production bindings. Add `--port` to set a custom port. State persists in `.wrangler/state/`. Test cron triggers via `curl http://localhost:8787/__scheduled`.

sections.title: Deploying
sections.body: `wrangler deploy` pushes the Worker to all 300+ Cloudflare edge locations. Use `--env <name>` for named environments. Reads configuration from `wrangler.toml`. The Worker URL is `<name>.<subdomain>.workers.dev` unless custom routes are configured. Use `wrangler rollback` to revert to a previous version.

sections.title: wrangler.toml configuration
sections.body: Required fields: `name` (worker name), `main` (entry file), `compatibility_date`. Optional: `account_id`, `compatibility_flags`, `routes`, `[vars]`, binding blocks (`[[kv_namespaces]]`, `[[r2_buckets]]`, `[[d1_databases]]`), `[triggers]` for crons, and `[env.<name>]` for named environments. Each environment can override any top-level setting.

sections.title: Live log streaming
sections.body: `wrangler tail <worker-name>` streams real-time logs from production Workers. Filter with `--status error`, `--search <text>`, or `--method POST`. Output as JSON with `--format json` for piping. Tail connects via WebSocket and shows `console.log()` output, exceptions, and request metadata.

related.note: Workers are the primary workload managed by Wrangler
Vercel CLI (vercel) is the equivalent for the Vercel platform

scripts.name: Wrangler Setup
scripts.description: Install Wrangler, authenticate, and verify the setup

depends.reason: API token required for most Wrangler operations
